text:
The mechanism by which copper-catalyzed cross-coupling reactions cleave carbon-halogen bonds has long been a subject of debate, largely due to the transient nature of high-valent copper intermediates and the complex redox pathways available to the metal. We report the synthesis and full characterization of stable alkyl Cu(III) complexes generated through the oxidative addition of $\alpha$-haloacetonitriles to both ionic and neutral Cu(I) precursors. This rare stability is achieved by employing strong-field trifluoromethyl ligands, which increase the kinetic barrier for reductive elimination. 

Mechanistic investigations, supported by kinetic analysis and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, reveal that the reaction pathway is highly dependent on the nature of the copper precursor. While the ionic cuprate reacts primarily via an $S_N2$-type substitution, the neutral bipyridine-ligated complex undergoes oxidative addition through a halogen-atom transfer (XAT) mechanism. Notably, we observed significant ligand acceleration in the neutral system, mirroring the reactivity patterns seen in practical copper-catalyzed couplings of alkyl electrophiles with various nucleophiles. These findings provide direct evidence for a Cu(I)/Cu(III) redox manifold in alkyl cross-couplings and offer a framework for the rational design of more efficient copper catalysts.
